story:
I used to curl in the armchair behind the sash window, sun on my tabby fur, ears tuned to birdsong. People insist witches’ cats are black, but I was jade-eyed and striped, “hiding in plain sight,” as Herself liked to say. She hid too: a mild-mannered academic with hair clipped back, only fired up when lecturing on the European Witch Hunt to bored Second Years.

I was born in the shed behind her house, one of four strays tumbling in leaves. Our elegant mother was killed by a roaring black four-by-four; we watched in shock from under the hedge. Herself took pity on the smallest—me—and fed me in the porch. I stayed, without surrendering my independence. She trained me as her familiar. My nose judged potions better than hers; I fetched small creatures and herbs from our patch by the back door. The Professor, absorbed in books and computer, noticed little. Her son once overheard us and joked we were discussing philosophy. Mostly, we planned excursions on the hazel broomstick—a bicycle saddle for her, a plank for me—hidden in the shed. On moonlit nights we slipped past the Professor’s snores and climbed the dark like birds.

We looped above the beech trees, saluted other witches and their familiars—some laughing, some almost transparent—and flew to fires in forest clearings. We danced to spectral violins, breathed woodsmoke and pine. The Sisterhood gave talks—wand annealing, broom maintenance, even spells against global warming. When Herself went vegetarian, she shared “recipes for the vegetarian witch.” I befriended a few cats; we sang to the moon, a-cat-pella.

We played harmless pranks. Mat O’Leary was drunk at the wheel; we whooshed his van into a swerve so the squad car stopped him before he harmed anyone. Imelda Moriarty tipped rubbish over our fence; that night I dabbed my right paw in powdered charcoal and traced art across her new blue walls. Mostly, though, we used our craft to help: nudging shy sweethearts with a drop of courage, stalling desperate feet on a bridge long enough for the sadness to pass, swaddling widows in gossamer memories, letting bullies feel, briefly, what fear is.

We did err. Testing a new vegan potion, my tail flicked the cauldron and the brew went fluorescent pink—so did my tail. “I’ll find you in the dark,” Herself laughed. I hissed and hid on a pink cushion until the St John’s Wort bloomed again. The Professor never noticed; people seldom register what they don’t expect.

We lived happily for years: me on her lap as she wrote, fish dinners, moonlit flights. The Professor disliked me. “I’m not a cat person,” he’d say, offended if I purred on her knees at dinner. He’d scrape back his chair and retreat, and she’d sigh and wash the dishes.

Then she won a fellowship in Australia—six weeks. She stocked my food, labeled his meals, and whispered we’d soon retire together. He promised to care for me and failed. He forgot my bowl, let my water cloud, refused the litter tray. When I left the smallest hint beside it, he shouted and kicked me out—cruel, and wrong. I hunted what I could, but I was old. By the time she returned, I had slipped under the beeches to die.

She called; I answered, twining at her calves, but she couldn’t hear. Seasons blurred. One bright spring morning she piled branches for a bonfire and brought out our broomstick. Only fire can end a witch’s broom. I rushed to her. She started, dropped the stick, sensed me, and wept, reaching; her hand passed through air. We talked a long time. Now she weeds to hide our visits; neighbors say she talks to herself. She kept the broom, though age and aches ground her flights.

I remain among the beech shadows—green in summer, russet in winter—waiting for her. When the time comes, we’ll rise together again, wind at our backs, skimming the treetops in the moonlight.
